name: OrCam MyEye
short_description: Wearable AI camera that clips to eyeglasses and reads text, identifies faces, and describes products aloud for blind and low-vision users.
long_description: OrCam MyEye is a finger-sized camera unit that attaches magnetically to any eyeglass frame and runs all processing on-device without requiring internet connectivity. It reads printed and digital text aloud, recognizes stored faces, identifies products by barcode, and narrates currency denominations — delivering output as speech through a small built-in speaker or Bluetooth headphone. Launched commercially in 2015 by Israeli company OrCam Technologies (co-founded by Professor Amnon Shashua and Ziv Aviram, who also co-founded Mobileye), it brought standalone, on-body computer vision to the blind and low-vision community as a consumer product, establishing a template for head-worn assistive AI.
ai_note: On-device computer vision powers OCR text reading, face recognition, barcode/object identification, and currency detection — the device does nothing without its AI stack.

images.caption: OrCam MyEye device resting against a pair of glasses on an open book, showing the small clip-on camera unit.
images.alt_text: A small black rectangular camera device labeled OrCam MyEye leans against a pair of dark-framed eyeglasses, both resting on the open pages of a printed book.

interactions.title: Point a finger to start reading
interactions.description: Touch a fingertip to a page or label with the nail facing up; the device frames the spot, chirps to confirm, and reads the text aloud after you pull your hand away.

interactions.title: Teach it a face, then hear who walked in
interactions.description: Hold the trigger button for two seconds while looking at someone and record their name; afterward the device speaks that name whenever the person comes into view, and it can pick out up to eight faces at once in a room.

interactions.title: Train your own grocery shortcut
interactions.description: Point at a product three times from different distances and backgrounds, record a short spoken label, and the device will announce that item by your own description the next time you pick it up — with room for up to 150 stored products.

interactions.title: Skim a newspaper by voice
interactions.description: Instead of hearing a page read top to bottom, say "Read the headlines" to scan the titles, then "Read the article about" a topic to jump straight into the piece you want.

interactions.title: Pull just the prices off a menu
interactions.description: Aim at a restaurant menu and say "Read the amounts" to hear only the prices, or "Read the dates" and "Read the phone numbers" to extract those fields from a document without sitting through the rest.

interactions.title: Check which bill is in your hand
interactions.description: Hold up a banknote or press the trigger and the device names the currency denomination from its pre-loaded set, so you can hand over the right cash at a register.

interactions.title: Read the room before you greet it
interactions.description: Walking into a gathering, ask how many people are present and hear a count, then let stored-face tags name the family and neighbors as they come into frame — using only the on-device people-counting and recognition the unit already runs.

interactions.title: Follow a slide deck from your seat
interactions.description: Glance up at a projected presentation and have each slide's text read into your ear as it changes, turning the same screen-text reading the device does for TVs and monitors into hands-free note-taking during a meeting.
